_meta.title: Concurrency vs Parallelism — Get This Right

questions.question: A single-core CPU runs a web browser with 10 open tabs, rapidly switching between them so each tab makes progress. This system is:
options: Concurrent but not parallel — tabs are structured independently but only one runs at a time
Parallel but not concurrent — tabs execute simultaneously on the single core
Both concurrent and parallel — tabs are independent and execute at the same time
Neither — the tabs are just sequential programs being swapped by the OS
questions.explanation: The browser structures 10 independent tasks (tabs), which is concurrency. But with one core, only one task executes at any instant via time-slicing — no parallelism. This is the canonical example of the Pike distinction.

questions.question: A program spends 80% of its time in a perfectly parallelizable function and 20% in serial code. Using Amdahl's Law, what is the maximum theoretical speedup regardless of how many cores you add?
options: 5x — the reciprocal of the serial fraction (1 / 0.20)
1.25x — Amdahl's Law says the parallel fraction barely helps
80x — just give it 80 cores and the parallel part goes away
Infinite — with enough cores, the parallel part runs in zero time
questions.explanation: Amdahl's Law: S(N) = 1 / ((1-P) + P/N). As N→∞, S → 1/(1-P) = 1/0.20 = 5x. The serial 20% fundamentally limits the speedup. This is why optimizing the serial bottleneck matters more than adding cores.

questions.question: Match the concurrency model to its communication mechanism: (1) Processes, (2) Async/await, (3) Actors, (4) CSP
options: IPC — Futures — Message passing — Synchronous channels
Message passing — IPC — Synchronous channels — Futures
Synchronous channels — Futures — IPC — Message passing
Futures — IPC — Message passing — Synchronous channels
questions.explanation: Processes communicate via IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory). Async/await uses futures/promises. Actors use asynchronous message passing through mailboxes. CSP uses synchronous channels where sender blocks until receiver is ready.

questions.question: A developer parallelizes a computation across 64 cores. Each work item takes 50 ns, and the synchronization barrier at the end of each iteration takes 500 ns. What is the dominant problem?
options: Synchronization overhead dominates — the sync cost (500 ns) is 10x the work per item (50 ns), so parallelism hurts throughput
This is perfectly fine — 64 cores give approximately 64x speedup
Memory bandwidth saturation — all 64 cores reading simultaneously will saturate the bus
Amdahl's Law — the serial fraction of the sync will limit speedup to about 10x
questions.explanation: The work-to-sync ratio is 50:500 = 0.1. Adding parallelism here adds cost (synchronization) that exceeds the computation. The rule of thumb is that work per sync should be > 10,000 cycles. This is a textbook case of when NOT to parallelize.

questions.question: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
options: They are the same thing
Concurrency is about dealing with multiple things at once (structure); parallelism is about doing multiple things at once (execution)
Parallelism is always faster than concurrency
Concurrency requires multiple CPUs; parallelism does not
questions.explanation: Concurrency is a program structure — multiple tasks making progress by interleaving (one CPU can still be concurrent via time-slicing). Parallelism is simultaneous execution — multiple tasks literally running at the same time on multiple cores. You can have concurrency without parallelism.
